coredata.dc:title: Constructing matrix-free solid-state carbon dots with efficient room-temperature phosphorescence
coredata.dc:creator: He, Pinyi
Zhu, Yaqing
Bai, Jianliang
Qin, Fu
Wang, Xinyu
Wu, Shuang
Yu, Xu
Ren, Lili
coredata.dc:description: Efficient solid-state room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) carbon dots (CDs) usually need to be embedded by a matrix to suppress the vibration dissipation between triplet and singlet states. However, selecting a suitable matrix is time-consuming, and the preparation process is often complicated. Here, we adopted a simple one-step hydrothermal method to obtain matrix-free solid-state RTP CDs with a long lifetime (1.22 s) and high fluorescence quantum yield (21.6%) by selecting suitable chain-like structure molecules for dehydration condensation and carbonization. The main source of RTP is the n-π* transition of CO/CN on the surface of CDs. The polymer chain structure effectively avoids the disordered collision of CDs during curing. The formed cross-linked network structure helps stabilize the excited triplet state and provides a rigid environment to prevent the triplet state excitons from being quenched by ambient oxygen. The facile preparation process and efficient RTP properties provide a new idea for researching and developing matrix-free CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Matrix-free
Room-temperature phosphorescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM of CDs; (b) HRTEM of CDs; (c) Particle size distribution of CDs.
High-resolution of (a) C1s, (b) O1s and (c) N1 spectra of CDs; (d) FT-IR spectra of CDs.
(a) UV–vis spectrum of CDs solution (blue line) and fluorescence spectrum under 365 nm excitation (red line); (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs solution under different excitations.
The pictures of CDs powder under sunlight, UV lamping, and UV lamping off.

(a) UV–vis spectrum (blue line), excitation (gray line) and emission spectrum (red line) of CDs powder; (b) Fluorescence spectra of CDs powder under different excitations; (c) Phosphorescence spectra of CDs powder under different excitations; (d) The lifetime of CDs powder.

(a) A schematic illustration of the structure of CDs in aqueous solution; (b) A schematic illustration of the structure of solid-state CDs; (c) Proposed fluorescence and phosphorescence emission processes of the CDs aqueous solution and powder.
Synthesis route of matrix-free RTP CDs.

head.ce:title: Constructing matrix-free solid-state carbon dots with efficient room-temperature phosphorescence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing, 211189, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing, 211189, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Efficient solid-state room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) carbon dots (CDs) usually need to be embedded by a matrix to suppress the vibration dissipation between triplet and singlet states. However, selecting a suitable matrix is time-consuming, and the preparation process is often complicated. Here, we adopted a simple one-step hydrothermal method to obtain matrix-free solid-state RTP CDs with a long lifetime (1.22 s) and high fluorescence quantum yield (21.6%) by selecting suitable chain-like structure molecules for dehydration condensation and carbonization. The main source of RTP is the n-π* transition of CO/CN on the surface of CDs. The polymer chain structure effectively avoids the disordered collision of CDs during curing. The formed cross-linked network structure helps stabilize the excited triplet state and provides a rigid environment to prevent the triplet state excitons from being quenched by ambient oxygen. The facile preparation process and efficient RTP properties provide a new idea for researching and developing matrix-free CDs.

A simple one-step hydrothermal method for preparing matrix-free solid-state carbon dots with ultralong-lifetime room-temperature phosphorescence (1.22 s) and high fluorescence quantum yield (21.6%).
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials utilize the radiative transition from the triple excited state to the ground state, which makes the luminescence lifetime several seconds or even longer. Therefore, compared with traditional fluorescent materials, RTP materials have more significant advantages, especially in some specific applications, including advanced anti-counterfeiting and encryption [1], in vivo phosphorescence imaging [2,3], optoelectronic devices [4,5], and so on. Currently, the efficient RTP materials are mainly concentrated on metal-based complexes and pure organic compounds [6–11]. However, due to the limitations of high cost, high toxicity, and complicated preparation process, it is urgent to develop a new environment-friendly and low-cost RTP material.

As a new type of luminescent nanoparticles, carbon dots (CDs) have many advantages, such as abundant raw materials, low cost, low toxicity, and simple synthetic methods [12–15]. Therefore, it has received extensive attention from researchers. At present, most studies embed CDs in suitable matrices to achieve RTP emission. Deng et al. [16] obtained CDs by pyrolysis and dispersed them into a polyvinyl alcohol matrix. When excited by UV light, obvious phosphorescence could be observed. The polyvinyl alcohol matrix effectively protected the triplet excited state from being quenched by intramolecular motion and ambient oxygen. Tan et al. [17] synthesized CDs by a microwave-assisted method and mixed them with polyurethane to obtain a composite with RTP properties. It was proved that the polyurethane matrix played a key role in suppressing the nonradiative transition of triplet excitons. Li et al. [18] synthesized nitrogen-doped CDs by solvothermal method and then embedded them in a composite matrix of urea and biuret. The RTP emission of CDs was realized because the composite matrix greatly suppressed the vibrational dissipation of the long-lived triplet state. The introduction of the matrix can effectively isolate ambient oxygen, limit the intermolecular vibration, and suppress the non-radiative transition of triplet excitons, making it possible for CDs to achieve RTP. However, selecting a suitable matrix is time-consuming, and the preparation process is often complicated, severely limiting CDs’ further development.

In addition to the strategy of introducing a matrix to realize solid-state RTP, the research on matrix-free RTP CDs has also been gradually developed in recent years. Jiang et al. [19] used succinic acid and diethylenetriamine as precursors and obtained matrix-free RTP CDs by solvothermal method (270 °C). The higher reaction temperature could help to generate a more compact structure and form hydrogen bonds, which effectively stabilized the excited triplet state of the CDs. Tao et al. [20] used polyacrylic acid and ethylenediamine as starting materials to prepare matrix-free RTP CDs by the one-step hydrothermal method. It has been proved that the structure of amide or imide and the crosslink-enhanced emission effect might contribute to the generation of RTP. The highly cross-linked polymer chain structure can limit the vibration and rotation of molecules and prevent disordered collisions between molecules. So, it plays a similar role as the matrix, which not only increases the effective intersystem crossing process but also improves the radiation recombination efficiency of triplet excitons. It provides favorable conditions for the realization of matrix-free RTP. Currently, the research on matrix-free RTP CDs is still in the initial stage. Complex preparation methods and easy quenching by ambient water and oxygen severely limit their industrialization. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a simple, rapid, and environment-friendly strategy for preparing matrix-free, ultralong-lifetime RTP CDs.

Herein, we selected pentasodium diethylenetriaminepentaacetate (DTPA-Na) and glycine (Gly) as starting materials to prepare matrix-free solid-state CDs by a simple one-step hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ). Since the DTPA-Na molecule has sufficient nitrogen and oxygen functional groups and an abundant branched chain structure, the chain molecules will collide during the polymerization process, and dehydration condensation occurs between different functional groups. Finally, large numbers of polymer chains are formed. These polymer chains can be cross-linked by covalent and hydrogen bonds. This may not only overcome the aggregation-induced quenching effect and prevent the quenching of excitons by ambient oxygen but also effectively suppress nonradiative transitions, which will guarantee the realization of matrix-free RTP. Thus, the fluorescence and phosphorescence properties of these matrix-free CDs were investigated, and the mechanism was also further discussed by structural characterization. This work will provide a new idea for the realization of matrix-free RTP CDs.

DTPA-Na 50% aqueous solution and Gly were purchased from TCI (Shanghai) Chemical Industry Development Co., Ltd. Deionized water was obtained by the Milli-Q water purification equipment. The reagents used in the experiments were all analytical grade without further purification.

1 mmol of DTPA-Na 50 wt % aqueous solution (1.06 g) was mixed with 1 mmol of Gly (0.75 g) and dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water. It was then transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined reactor and placed in an oven at 200 °C for 5 h. After the reaction was completed, it was cooled to room temperature naturally. A clear and transparent CDs solution was obtained. Unreacted molecules and larger particles of impurities were removed by vacuum filtration through a filter paper membrane (0.22 μm). Finally, the solution was freeze-dried to obtain a white powder.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained by a Talos F200X microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded with a Nicolet 5700 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was conducted on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI instrument. Zeta potential was measured by a Dispersion Technology Instrument DT310. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded on a Rigaku Ultima IV diffractometer. Raman spectrum was measured on a Horiba LabRAM HR Evolution spectrometer. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on a Horiba Fluoromax-4 fluorescence spectrometer. The FL-QY and RTP decay curves were measured by a Fluorolog-3 spectrofluorometer.

In order to understand the morphology and structural characteristics of CDs, their structures were characterized by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the CDs were uniformly dispersed and spherical with an average particle size of about 2.8 nm (Fig. 1c). HRTEM (Fig. 1b) shows that the CDs have distinct and resolvable lattice fringes with an interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [21].

To clarify the structure, surface composition, crystallinity, and charge amount of CDs, XPS, FT-IR, Raman, XRD, and Zeta potential were performed, respectively. As shown in Fig. S1, CDs are mainly composed of C, N, O, and Na elements. From the high-resolution C 1s spectra (Fig. 2 a), the peaks can be divided into the C–N/C–O peak at 285.92 eV, the N–CO peak at 288.03 eV, and the C–C/CC peak at 284.8 eV. The high-resolution N 1s spectra (Fig. 2b) show an N–H peak at 401.37 eV and a C–NC peak at 399.48 eV. The high-resolution O 1s spectra (Fig. 2c) consists of a C–OH peak at 531.12 eV and a CO peak at 534.87 eV [19,22–24]. In the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2d), it is found that there is a broad characteristic absorption peak at 3400 cm−1 and an absorption peak at 3190 cm−1, which is the characteristic stretching vibration peak of N–H in the amide. The absorption peaks at 1440 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration peaks of C–N and CO, respectively, while the peak at 1540 cm−1 can be attributed to the bending vibration of N–H [19,20,25]. These fully indicate the existence of amide functional groups in the structure of CDs, which is consistent with the analysis in XPS characterization, indicating that dehydration polymerization occurs between the reactants, and then amides are formed. The zeta potential of CDs is +45.5 mV (Fig. S2), indicating that the highly positively charged functional groups presented over the CDs' surface imparted higher stability and dispersibility [26–28]. The XRD pattern (Fig. S3a) shows an obvious diffraction peak at 24°, which corresponds to the (002) lattice plane of graphitic carbon, indicating that the CDs have a certain graphitic structure [29,30]. The Raman spectrum (Fig. S3b) exhibits two typical graphitic bands (D and G) at 1416 and 1553 cm−1, respectively. The calculated IG/ID of 1.1 indicates that CDs have a certain degree of graphitization [19,31]. The above characterization results show that the surface of CDs contains abundant nitrogen and oxygen functional groups, which are beneficial to promoting the intersystem crossing of excitons. The –OH/-NH on the surface can also effectively construct the hydrogen bond framework and stabilize the excited triplet state.

The prepared CDs aqueous solution was clear and transparent before drying into powder and gradually turned yellowish as the solution was concentrated. The solution exhibited blue fluorescence under the UV lamp at 365 nm, and there was no phosphorescence when the UV lamp was turned off. Unlike the previously prepared CDs, the increase in solution concentration did not lead to fluorescence quenching, and the concentration did not have a significant influence on the fluorescence intensity. Fig. 3 a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the CDs solution. The strong absorption peak at 225 nm can be attributed to the π-π* transition of CC and the weak absorption peaks at 266 and 328 nm are assigned to the n-π* transition of CO/CN [18]. The spectra indicate that the structures of CDs may be unsaturated aliphatic compounds rather than aromatic compounds. The fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3b) exhibit excitation wavelength-dependent properties in the blue emission region.

The CDs aqueous solution was freeze-dried to obtain a white powder. As shown in Fig. 4 , under the irradiation of a UV lamp at 365 nm, the powder emits strong blue fluorescence, and its FL-QY is measured to be 21.6%, which is the highest among the reported matrix-free CDs (Table S1). When the UV lamp is turned off, the powder emits a green phosphorescence for about 7 s.

Fig. 5 a shows the UV–Vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of solid-state CDs. The strong absorption peak at 211 nm is attributed to the π-π* transition of CC, and the characteristic absorption peaks at 269 and 340 nm can be attributed to the n-π* transitions of CO/CN [18]. When the emission wavelength is 425 nm, the peak of the corresponding excitation spectrum coincides with the absorption peak at 340 nm, indicating that the fluorescence of CDs comes from the n-π* transition of CO/CN. The fluorescence spectrum shows that solid-state CDs have the same excitation wavelength-dependent characteristics as liquid-state, indicating that the fluorescence sources are the same, and changing their material state does not change their luminescence source (Figs. 3b and 5b). The phosphorescence spectra of solid-state CDs (Fig. 5c) showed no excitation wavelength-dependent properties, and the emission peak was fixed at 500 nm. The average decay lifetime of solid-state CDs was calculated from Equation (1), which is 1.22 s after fitting with a three-exponential function (Fig. 5d and Table S2), indicating that there are multiple channels for phosphorescence decay. The ultralong phosphorescence lifetime will have great potential in the fields of information encryption and anti-counterfeiting.
(1)
τ
a v g
= ∑ α i τ
i 2
/ ∑ α i τ i

Based on the above discussion of the structure and optical properties, it is believed that the source of efficient RTP is closely related to the selection of starting materials and the formation process of CDs. Firstly, DTPA-NA is a potential molecule for the synthesis of RTP CDs due to its sufficient nitrogen and oxygen functional groups and abundant branched chain structure. The Gly molecule has two functional groups: amino and carboxyl. The amino group is a highly efficient sub-fluorophore, which can dominate the fluorescence emission of non-conjugated polymer CDs [32]. Meanwhile, the amino group can provide effective nitrogen doping and achieve the cross-linking of long-chain structures by dehydration condensation with carboxyl functional groups. With the gradual intensification of cross-linking polymerization, the structure becomes more compact and stable [33–37]. Finally, the dendritic polymer CDs are formed (Fig. 6 a). Secondly, both DTPA-Na and Gly molecules have hydrogen bond formation sites. From the characterization results of FTIR and XPS, it can be seen that the surface of CDs has functional groups such as –NH/-OH and carboxyl groups, which are conducive to forming hydrogen bonds between polymer chains. Due to the fixation of hydrogen bonds, the disordered collision of CDs during curing is effectively avoided, and the triplet exciton is protected from quenching by ambient water and oxygen [29,38], thus achieving efficient RTP (Fig. 6b).

Fig. 6c presents the emission process of fluorescence and RTP of CDs aqueous solution and powder. Due to the destruction of hydrogen bonds between polymer chains by water, the aqueous solution of CDs only emits blue fluorescence without phosphorescence under UV excitation. Under the dual effects of polymer chain cross-linking and hydrogen bond fixation, CDs powder not only overcomes the aggregation-induced quenching effect but also prevents ambient water and oxygen, which promotes the radiative recombination of triplet excitons [39–41], and finally obtains efficient fluorescence and RTP.

In summary, we obtained the efficient matrix-free RTP CDs by a simple one-step hydrothermal method. The matrix-free CDs have an ultralong-lifetime RTP (1.22 s) and a high FL-QY (21.6%), and the green phosphorescence visible to the naked eye is up to 7 s, with efficient phosphorescence and fluorescence properties. Cross-linked polymer chains and hydrogen bonds on the surface of CDs effectively stabilize the excited triplet state and prevent the quenching of excitons by ambient oxygen. The oxygen and nitrogen functional groups on the long chain of the surface provide a guarantee for the realization of RTP. This work is of great significance for promoting the research and development of matrix-free RTP CDs.

Pinyi He: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing-original draft, Writing-review and editing, Visualization, Formal analysis, Investigation; Yaqing Zhu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization, Investigation; Jianliang Bai: Methodology, Software, Validation; Fu Qin: Methodology, Software; Xinyu Wang: Visualization, Formal analysis; Shuang Wu: Formal analysis; Xu Yu: Visualization; Lili Ren: Resources, Supervision, Writing-review and editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Research platform; All authors contributed to the discussion.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22275035).
